name: Margarita
history: The most popular tequila cocktail in the United States. Its exact origin is disputed, with claims spanning 1930s–40s Mexico and the Texas border; the name means "daisy" in Spanish. It is a defining drink of Tex-Mex bars, especially along the border near El Paso.

name: Paloma
history: Mexico's most popular tequila cocktail — far more common there than the margarita. It is traditionally built with grapefruit soda such as Squirt or Jarritos. "Paloma" means "dove" in Spanish.

name: Ranch Water
garnish: Lime wedge (Topo Chico, El Paso style)
history: A West Texas highball of tequila, lime, and Topo Chico mineral water. It was born in the Trans-Pecos region around Fort Davis and Marfa and is beloved across far West Texas, including El Paso.

name: Tequila Sunrise
history: Named for the layered colors that form as grenadine settles to the bottom. The modern version was created in the early 1970s in Sausalito, California, and spread widely after the Rolling Stones adopted it on their 1972 tour.

name: Tequila Soda
history: A simple, light highball of tequila and soda water with a squeeze of lime. It has become a modern bar standard for drinkers wanting something crisp and low in sugar.

name: Mexican Mule
history: A tequila twist on the Moscow Mule, swapping vodka for tequila alongside ginger beer and lime. It is sometimes called a "Burro" and is a natural fit anywhere mules and margaritas meet.

name: Vodka Cranberry
history: Also known as the Cape Codder, named for Cape Cod's cranberry bogs. It was popularized by Ocean Spray in the mid-20th century, originally marketed as the "Red Devil."

name: Screwdriver
history: Vodka and orange juice, said to be named by American oil workers in the Persian Gulf who stirred the drink with their screwdrivers. It has been a brunch and bar staple since the 1950s.

name: Vodka Soda
history: The quintessential minimalist highball — just vodka, soda water, and a squeeze of lime. It is a perennial go-to for a clean, low-sugar drink.

name: Vodka Tonic
history: A lighter cousin of the gin and tonic. The quinine in tonic water gives it a crisp, faintly bitter edge that pairs cleanly with vodka.

name: Cosmopolitan
history: A pink vodka cocktail that surged in popularity through the 1990s and was cemented in pop culture by "Sex and the City." It balances vodka and triple sec with cranberry and lime.

name: Sea Breeze
history: A vodka highball with cranberry and grapefruit juice that became a hit in the 1980s. It is tart, refreshing, and named for breezy summer days by the water.

name: Bay Breeze
history: A close relative of the Sea Breeze that swaps grapefruit for pineapple juice, giving it a softer, sweeter tropical profile.

name: Greyhound
history: Vodka and grapefruit juice, dating to the 1930s. Add a salted rim and it becomes its close cousin, the Salty Dog.

name: Madras
history: Vodka with cranberry and orange juice, named after the colorful madras plaid fabric for its reddish-orange hue.

name: Moscow Mule
history: Vodka, ginger beer, and lime, traditionally served in a copper mug. It was invented in 1941 in Los Angeles as a clever way to sell both vodka and ginger beer.

name: Gin and Tonic
history: Born in British colonial India, where tonic's quinine helped fight malaria and gin made the medicine palatable. It has since become a global classic.

name: Tom Collins
history: A tall, fizzy mix of gin, lemon, sugar, and soda. Its name traces to an 1874 New York hoax about a fictitious gossip named Tom Collins.

name: Gin Rickey
history: A dry, unsweetened highball of gin, lime, and soda. It was created in 1880s Washington, D.C., and became a favorite of the Jazz Age.

name: Cuba Libre
history: Rum and cola with lime, born in Cuba around 1900 after Coca-Cola arrived on the island. The name means "Free Cuba," a toast dating to the Spanish-American War era.

name: Daiquiri
history: Rum, lime, and sugar, named for a beach near Santiago de Cuba. A favorite of Ernest Hemingway, it serves as the template for countless variations.

name: Pina Colada
history: Puerto Rico's national drink, blending rum, pineapple, and coconut cream. It was invented in San Juan in the 1950s–60s; the name means "strained pineapple."

name: Dark and Stormy
history: Dark rum floated over ginger beer, originating in Bermuda. Gosling's holds a trademark on the name, made with their Black Seal rum.

name: Whiskey and Coke
history: A straightforward highball pairing whiskey with cola. Ubiquitous in American bars, its most famous form is the "Jack and Coke."

name: Whiskey Sour
history: Whiskey, lemon, and sugar, documented since the 1860s. It is a timeless template — balanced, tart, and easy to love.

name: Whiskey and Ginger
history: Whiskey lengthened with ginger ale, often called a "Jack and Ginger." It is crisp, easygoing, and endlessly popular.

name: Long Island Iced Tea
history: A potent mix of five spirits with a splash of cola that mimics the look of iced tea. Most accounts credit a bartender on Long Island, New York, in the 1970s.

name: Shirley Temple
history: A non-alcoholic favorite of lemon-lime soda and grenadine, finished with a cherry. It was named for the child film star in the 1930s.

name: Roy Rogers
history: The cola-based, non-alcoholic counterpart to the Shirley Temple, named for the singing cowboy actor Roy Rogers.
